docs.title: Welcome to the Drone Project Documentation
docs.text:
This project was initiated in 2025 by students of the Control & Robotics program at Université Claude Bernard Lyon 1.

With no constraints other than time, our goal was simple: push the boundaries of what we could build, and experiment hands-on with state-of-the-art techniques and algorithms developed for autonomous drones.

This documentation walks you through the full journey — where we started, what we built, the problems we solved, and the ones we didn't. And for everything time didn't allow us to finish, you'll find the perspectives and directions we hoped to explore.

To whoever picks this up next — the project is yours now. Build further.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The drone is an underactuated system: it has 4 control inputs (rotor speeds) but 6 degrees of freedom (3 translational, 3 rotational). As a result, it is impossible to independently control all positions and orientations simultaneously. Our control strategy therefore targets 4 degrees of freedom: the 3D position \(p^w\) and the yaw angle \(\psi\).

Two control architectures were studied and implemented:

Controller Approach Suitable for PD (near-hovering) Linearization around hover Small angle maneuvers Geometric SE(3) Nonlinear, SO(3)-based Aggressive maneuvers

The geometric controller is the primary controller used in this project.

docs.title: Dynamic Model
docs.text:
The quadrotor dynamics are described by the Newton-Euler equations for a rigid body:

\[\begin{bmatrix} f^w \\ \tau^B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} mI_3 & 0 \\ 0 & J \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a^w \\ \alpha^B \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -mg^w \\ \omega^B \times J\omega^B \end{bmatrix}\]

Expanding with the forces and torques generated by the rotors, the full model becomes:

\[\begin{bmatrix} m\dot{v}^w \\ J\dot{\omega}^B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -mge_3 \\ -\omega^B \times J\omega^B \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} R^w_B & 0 \\ 0 & I_3 \end{bmatrix} F\mathbf{w}\]

where \(\mathbf{w} = [w_1|w_1|,\ w_2|w_2|,\ w_3|w_3|,\ w_4|w_4|]^T\) contains the signed squared rotor velocities, and \(F\) is the allocation matrix mapping rotor inputs to forces and torques:

\[\begin{bmatrix} f_x^B \\ f_y^B \\ f_z^B \\ \tau_x^B \\ \tau_y^B \\ \tau_z^B \end{bmatrix} = F\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} c_f \mathbf{e}_3 & c_f \mathbf{e}_3 & c_f \mathbf{e}_3 & c_f \mathbf{e}_3 \\ c_d \mathbf{e}_3 + c_f \boldsymbol{\rho}_1^B \times \mathbf{e}_3 & -c_d \mathbf{e}_3 + c_f \boldsymbol{\rho}_2^B \times \mathbf{e}_3 & c_d \mathbf{e}_3 + c_f \boldsymbol{\rho}_3^B \times \mathbf{e}_3 & -c_d \mathbf{e}_3 + c_f \boldsymbol{\rho}_4^B \times \mathbf{e}_3 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{w}\]

The state of the system is \((p^w,\ v^w,\ R^w_B,\ \omega^B)\) and evolves according to:

\[\dot{p}^w = v^w, \qquad \dot{R}^w_B = R^w_B [\omega^B]_\times\]

docs.title: Geometric Controller on SE(3)
docs.text: The geometric controller was originally introduced by Lee, Leok, and McClamroch (2010). It is a nonlinear controller defined directly on the Lie group \(SE(3) = SO(3) \times \mathbb{R}^3\), which avoids the singularities associated with Euler angle representations (gimbal lock). Unlike PD or backstepping controllers, it supports aggressive maneuvers such as recovery from an inverted state.

docs.title: Control Architecture
docs.text: The controller takes as inputs the desired position \(p^w_d\) and a desired yaw direction \(\tilde{x}^w_d = [\cos\psi_d,\ \sin\psi_d,\ 0]^T\), and outputs the total thrust \(f^B_z\) and the control torque \(\tau^B\).

docs.title: Tracking Errors
docs.text:
The errors are defined on the appropriate manifolds:

\[e_p = p^w - p^w_d \qquad \text{(position error)}\] \[e_v = v^w - v^w_d \qquad \text{(velocity error)}\] \[e_R = \frac{1}{2}\left((R^w_d)^T R^w_B - (R^w_B)^T R^w_d\right)^\vee \qquad \text{(rotation error on SO(3))}\] \[e_\omega = \omega^B - (R^w_B)^T R^w_d\, \omega_d \qquad \text{(angular velocity error)}\]

The rotation error \(e_R\) is a vector parametrization of the relative rotation between the current attitude \(R^w_B\) and the desired attitude \(R^w_d\). It is derived from the SO(3) log map and vanishes when the two frames are aligned.

docs.title: Desired Attitude Construction
docs.text:
The desired third body axis \(z^w_d\) is constructed from the position controller output:

\[z^w_d = \frac{-k_p e_p - k_v e_v + mge_3 + m\ddot{p}^w_d}{\lVert -k_p e_p - k_v e_v + mge_3 + m\ddot{p}^w_d \rVert}\]

The full desired rotation matrix is then assembled as:

\[R^w_d = \left[\frac{(z^w_d \times \tilde{x}^w_d) \times z^w_d}{\lVert(z^w_d \times \tilde{x}^w_d) \times z^w_d\rVert},\quad \frac{z^w_d \times \tilde{x}^w_d}{\lVert z^w_d \times \tilde{x}^w_d \rVert},\quad z^w_d\right] \in SO(3)\]

docs.title: Control Laws
docs.text:
Thrust:

\[f^B_z = \left(-k_p e_p - k_v e_v + mge_3 + m\ddot{p}^w_d\right) \cdot R^w_B e_3\]

The dot product with \(R^w_B e_3\) projects the desired force onto the current body \(z\)-axis. When there is an attitude error, the thrust magnitude is naturally reduced, preventing instability.

Torque:

\[\tau^B = -k_R e_R - k_\omega e_\omega + \omega^B \times J\omega^B - J\left([\omega^B]_\times (R^w_B)^T R^w_d\, \omega_d - (R^w_B)^T R^w_d\, \dot{\omega}_d\right)\]

This torque law exponentially stabilizes the attitude tracking error on \(SO(3)\).

docs.title: Gain Summary
docs.text: Gain Controls Effect \(k_p\) Position error \(e_p\) Stiffness of position response \(k_v\) Velocity error \(e_v\) Damping of translational dynamics \(k_R\) Rotation error \(e_R\) Stiffness of attitude response \(k_\omega\) Angular velocity error \(e_\omega\) Damping of rotational dynamics

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
The geometric controller is implemented as a ROS 2 node (controller_node_visual) in C++, using the Eigen library for all linear algebra operations. It subscribes to:

  • /mavros/local_position/pose — current position and orientation (\(p^w\), \(R^w_B\))
  • /mavros/local_position/velocity_body — current body-frame velocity
  • Desired trajectory setpoints (position, velocity, acceleration, yaw)

And publishes:

  • /mavros/setpoint_raw/attitude — attitude setpoint and thrust command sent to ArduPilot via MAVROS

The allocation matrix inversion \(F^{-1}\) is handled internally by ArduPilot on the MiniPix flight controller.

docs.title: Comparison with PD Controller
docs.text:
The near-hovering PD controller is derived by linearizing the equations of motion around the hover equilibrium (\(\phi = \theta = 0\), \(\dot{p} = 0\)). It uses nested control loops for attitude and position, with Euler angles as the attitude representation.

While simple and computationally efficient, the PD controller:

  • Is only valid near hover (small roll and pitch angles)
  • Suffers from Euler angle singularities
  • Cannot perform aggressive maneuvers

The geometric controller lifts these restrictions by working directly on \(SO(3)\), at the cost of slightly higher implementation complexity.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• T. Lee, M. Leok, N. H. McClamroch. Geometric tracking control of a quadrotor UAV on SE(3). IEEE CDC, 2010.
  • D. Mellinger, V. Kumar. Minimum snap trajectory generation and control for quadrotors. IEEE ICRA, 2011.
  • L. Carlone. 16.485 VNAV Lecture Notes: Quadrotor Dynamics & Control. MIT, Fall 2023.

docs.title: Hardware Assembly & Components
docs.text:
This document outlines the physical build and electrical components of the drone.

The initial concept was based on a Parallax ELEV-8 kit. However, because the original chassis, flight controller, and motor mounts were missing, we custom-engineered replacement parts and upgraded the core electronics to support our specific payload and requirements.

Prototyping Status

As this is an active experimental build, several components are currently secured using zip ties and tape. This allows for rapid and easy component swapping.

docs.title: 1. Frame and Chassis Assembly
docs.text:
To replace the missing Parallax parts, we modeled a custom multi-level chassis in SolidWorks and manufactured it using laser cutting. The design uses spacers to separate the levels and distribute the hardware:

  • Main Body Stack: Consists of three identical, laser-cut chassis plates stacked on top of each other using standoffs. These lower plates house the heavy power distribution wiring and the ESCs.
  • Battery Placement: The 3S LiPo battery is strapped directly to the outer bottom face of the lowest chassis plate.
  • Avionics Plate: A smaller, dedicated chassis plate is mounted on the very top of the stack. This isolates the flight controller from the main power lines.

docs.title: 2. Motors, ESCs, and Propellers
docs.text:
Because we are adding a companion computer, we needed more lifting capacity than a standard ELEV-8. We sourced heavy-duty hardware from KDE Direct.

  • Motors: 4x KDE3510XF-475 (Brushless)
  • ESCs: 4x KDEXF-UAS55
  • Propellers: KDE-CF155-DP-M4 (15.5-inch folding carbon fiber blades)

docs.title: 3. Flight Controller & Avionics
docs.text:
The original Parallax control board was replaced with a system capable of running ArduPilot, allowing us to send MAVLink commands from a companion computer.

  • Flight Controller: Radiolink MiniPix (Mounted on the top avionics plate).
  • Receiver: Happymodel ELRS 2.4G EP2 TCXO.

Custom Receiver Wiring (TELEM 1)

The Radiolink MiniPix typically expects a standard receiver to be plugged into the RC IN port. However, our Happymodel ExpressLRS receiver uses a serial protocol. Instead of using RC IN, we bypassed it and wired the receiver directly to the TELEM1 serial port. This allows us to transmit both the pilot's control inputs and receive full MAVLink telemetry back to the controller over a single connection.

graph TD
  BATT[3S LiPo Battery] --> PDB[Power Distribution]
  BATT --> REG[5V Regulator]
  PDB --> ESC1[ESC 1: KDEXF-UAS55]
  PDB --> ESC2[ESC 2: KDEXF-UAS55]
  PDB --> ESC3[ESC 3: KDEXF-UAS55]
  PDB --> ESC4[ESC 4: KDEXF-UAS55]

  ESC1 --> M1[Motor 1: KDE3510XF-475]
  ESC2 --> M2[Motor 2: KDE3510XF-475]
  ESC3 --> M3[Motor 3: KDE3510XF-475]
  ESC4 --> M4[Motor 4: KDE3510XF-475]

  PDB -.->|5V Power| FC[Radiolink MiniPix]

  FC -.->|PWM Signal| ESC1
  FC -.->|PWM Signal| ESC2
  FC -.->|PWM Signal| ESC3
  FC -.->|PWM Signal| ESC4

  FC <-->|TELEM 1| RX[Happymodel EP2 TCXO]
  FC <-->|USB| RPI[Raspberry Pi 4]
  REG --> RPI
  RPI --- CAM[RPi Camera Module]

docs.title: 4. Companion Computer
docs.text:
To run the ROS 2 software stack, we are integrating an onboard computer.

  • Hardware: Raspberry Pi 4.
  • Vision: Standard Raspberry Pi Camera module.
  • Connection: The RPi 4 connects directly to the MiniPix flight controller via USB to send and receive MAVLink commands. Physical mounting for these components is currently being finalized.

docs.title: 5. Mechanical Assets & Manufacturing
docs.text:
The physical drone was manufactured using the specific 2D laser-cutting profiles below.

  • Download Main Chassis Plate (.dxf) - Profile used for the three main stacked body plates.
  • Download Controller Plate (.dxf) - Profile used for the smaller top plate holding the flight controller.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The ROS 2 stack runs entirely onboard the Raspberry Pi 4 companion computer, which is mounted on the drone frame and flies with it. The ground laptop is used for monitoring only — all control computation happens on the RPi.

docs.title: Topic Graph
docs.text: Publisher Topic Subscriber v4l2_camera /camera/image_raw aruco_detector_node aruco_detector_node /aruco/marker_centroid visual_servo_node mavros /mavros/local_position/odom visual_servo_node visual_servo_node /mavros/setpoint_raw/local mavros

docs.title: MAVROS
docs.text:
MAVROS is the translation bridge between ROS 2 and ArduPilot via MAVLink over UART.

Key topics:

Topic Type Direction /mavros/state mavros_msgs/State Read — connection status /mavros/local_position/odom nav_msgs/Odometry Read — pose + velocity /mavros/setpoint_raw/attitude mavros_msgs/AttitudeTarget Write — thrust + quaternion /mavros/setpoint_raw/local mavros_msgs/PositionTarget Write — velocity setpoints

Launch: 

ros2 launch mavros apm.launch fcu_url:=/dev/ttyAMA0:921600

Verify connection: 

ros2 topic echo /mavros/state
# connected: True

docs.title: Camera Driver
docs.text:
The downward-facing camera is exposed via v4l2_camera (USB) or raspicam2 (Pi Camera). Recommended resolution: 640×480 @ 30 FPS.

Calibrate with a checkerboard: 

ros2 run camera_calibration cameracalibrator \
  --size 8x6 --square 0.025 \
  --ros-args -r image:=/camera/image_raw

Target reprojection error: < 0.5 px. The resulting camera_info.yaml provides the focal length \(f\) needed for the pixel-to-metric conversion.

docs.title: ArUco Detector
docs.text:
The ros2_aruco package detects markers and publishes their centroid and pose.

Parameter Value Dictionary DICT_4X4_50 Primary marker size 40 cm Target detection rate ≥ 15 FPS on RPi

docs.title: SE3 Controller Node
docs.text:
The geometric SE(3) controller runs as a C++ ROS 2 node (controller_node_visual) at 100 Hz using Eigen for all linear algebra.

Subscriptions:

Topic Usage /mavros/local_position/odom Position \(p^w\), velocity \(v^w\) /mavros/local_position/pose Orientation \(R^w_B\) /aruco/marker_centroid Visual error \((e_u, e_v)\)

Publications:

Topic Usage /mavros/setpoint_raw/attitude Thrust \(f^B_z\) + attitude quaternion /se3/debug Debug array for PlotJuggler

Tunable parameters:

Parameter Default Description kp 1.0 Position gain kv 2.0 Velocity damping kR 1.5 Attitude gain kOmega 0.5 Angular velocity damping vmax 0.3 Max velocity command (m/s)

docs.title: Visual Servo Node
docs.text:
Converts the ArUco pixel error into metric velocity commands:

\[e_u = u_{\text{marker}} - u_0, \quad e_v = v_{\text{marker}} - v_0\] \[e_x = \frac{h}{f} e_u, \quad e_y = \frac{h}{f} e_v\] \[v^{\text{cmd}}_x = \text{clip}(k_x\, e_x,\ -v_{\text{max}},\ +v_{\text{max}})\] \[v^{\text{cmd}}_y = \text{clip}(k_y\, e_y,\ -v_{\text{max}},\ +v_{\text{max}})\]

where \(h\) is altitude from the barometer and \(f\) is the calibrated focal length.

If no marker is detected for more than 0.5 s, the node holds the last setpoint and publishes a warning on /rosout.

docs.text:
dc_ws/
├── src/
│   ├── se3_controller/
│   ├── visual_servo_node/

│   └── mavros/

docs.title: Debugging
docs.text:
Monitor all signals live with PlotJuggler: 

ros2 run plotjuggler plotjuggler

Check node health: 

ros2 node list
ros2 topic hz /mavros/local_position/odom
ros2 topic hz /se3/debug

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Trajectory generation determines how the drone moves between waypoints. Two approaches were studied and compared in simulation: the ArduPilot S-curve (native trajectory generator) and Minimum Snap polynomial optimization.

docs.title: ArduPilot S-Curve
docs.text:
ArduPilot's native trajectory generator produces jerk-limited S-curve profiles between waypoints. Compared to a trapezoidal velocity profile, the S-curve generates smoother commands and softer dynamics, reducing mechanical stress on the actuators.

However, it does not perform true velocity or time optimization. When multiple waypoints must be traversed under obstacle or time constraints, the S-curve becomes limiting — it cannot exploit the full dynamic envelope of the drone.

docs.title: Minimum Snap Optimization
docs.text:
The Minimum Snap approach generates smooth polynomial trajectories by minimizing the fourth derivative of position (snap) over the flat outputs \(\sigma = [x, y, z, \psi]^T\):

\[J(\sigma) = \int_0^T \left[ \left(x^{(4)}\right)^2 + \left(y^{(4)}\right)^2 + \left(z^{(4)}\right)^2 \right] dt + \int_0^T \left(\psi^{(2)}\right)^2 dt\]

This exploits the differential flatness property of the quadrotor: all states and inputs can be expressed as algebraic functions of \(\sigma\) and its derivatives, so any smooth trajectory in the flat output space is directly feasible for the drone without dynamic simulation.

The optimization is reformulated as a quadratic program (QP). Two formulations were evaluated:

Method Solver Time (ms) Constrained QP MATLAB quadprog 9.5 Constrained QP C++/Eigen 0.18 Unconstrained QP MATLAB 2.7 Unconstrained QP C++/Eigen 0.34

The unconstrained formulation was adopted for its numerical stability — high-order polynomials with many segments tend to produce near-singular matrices in the constrained case.

All linear algebra operations are implemented in C++ with Eigen.

docs.title: Simulation Comparison
docs.text: Both methods were tested in simulation using ROS 2, ArduPilot SITL, and Gazebo on a rectangular trajectory (3 m sides, 5 m altitude), returning to the start point.

docs.title: Flight Path
docs.text: The ArduPilot S-curve follows a path close to a square. The Minimum Snap trajectory produces smoother, more curved paths due to the high-degree polynomial formulation — intermediate waypoint constraints can be added to enforce sharper corners if required.

docs.title: Velocity
docs.text: Minimum Snap reduces total mission time by approximately 40% and increases peak velocity from 1.5 m/s to 2.3 m/s. Velocity oscillations visible in the ArduPilot profile result from the controller struggling to track the aggressive S-curve commands — these can be reduced with gain tuning.

docs.title: Acceleration
docs.text: The Minimum Snap acceleration profile is smoother and exhibits a slightly lower peak, resulting in reduced actuator stress and a more fluid dynamic response.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
The trajectory optimizer is implemented in C++ with Eigen and integrated as a ROS 2 node. Waypoints are defined as a list of positions; the optimizer computes the full polynomial trajectory and streams setpoints to the SE3 controller at runtime.

Key parameters:

Parameter Value Polynomial degree 7 Flat outputs \(x, y, z, \psi\) Solver Unconstrained QP (Eigen) Language C++

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• D. Mellinger, V. Kumar. Minimum Snap Trajectory Generation and Control for Quadrotors. IEEE ICRA, 2011.
  • C. Richter, A. Bry, N. Roy. Polynomial Trajectory Planning for Aggressive Quadrotor Flight in Dense Indoor Environments. Robotics Research, Springer, 2016.
